author: @amirrahiminia2556
text: Graphene is more absorbent version of Carbon. ammonia and carbonic acid both have ionic properties therefore it should work for all ionizable substances in air or liquid.  Would be interesting if it works to the same manner in pure water or it's vapor in the absence of CO2.

author: @keeganmaloney3440
text:
I cant think of a way to easily turn this into a product that could be made cheaper than mass produced products that do the same thing through established technologies. Without knowing what those technologies are, and only seeing that they run about $30 (usd) for a detector and $100 (usd) for a digital readout... and 15 for a replacement sensor...could you replace their sensor for yours?

Could that be cheaper than the one they produce? If so, than I'm sure you could sell your idea to one of those companies. It may be the only way to make this particular application financially feasible. I could be wrong, it could be that you could make the entire device on your own more cheaply than established mass production, I just don't know how someone could do that. The gross margin seems really tiny after the materials for the rest of the device, and then there's assembly and usually a few other costs associated with selling a product in enough volume to make a profit.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't think you could just replace the sensor mate - they tend to be calibrated systems - but the electronics to run this would probably be a couple of dollars - so yeah - you could make a cheap system really easily - but - It's not something I would want to do - the legislative and selling background to doing a product like this would be a nightmare

author: @MongrelShark
text:
Wow. You just keep coming up with cool things to do with graphene. This one is awesome.

If the response is liniar. It would not take long to write up some code for arduino or similar to give a read out. As long as a quality meter was available tor comparison to get the calibration right. Compensating for humidity would be a bit more involved, but not too hard, provided acurate readings where available from a trusted device. Worst case you could use 2 K-probes and have a wet cloth on one to get humidity ofset....

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am not quite sure how to post it on here but i have an interesting paper on how to use graphene as a multi gas sensor based on changes in both resistance and frequency responses if you want it drop me an email to robertmurraysmith64@gmail.com and I will reply back with the paper as an attachment

replies.author: @MongrelShark
replies.text:
Sounds interesting. Check your email :)

Google docs or google drive would be another way to share a document. With drive (and maybe docs) you can chose who to share it with if needed.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Mongrel Shark
check your own mail lol

replies.author: @MongrelShark
replies.text:
Had a quick read of the paper. Its very interesting. A spectrum analyser would be needed to identify gasses in this way. Although it is very very low frequency stuff. An arduino may be up to the task, many other cheap and open source processors would likely also be suitable.  The coding is a bit beyond what I know, but I'm fairly confident it could be done.
I wonder if the Graphene FET would really have to be on such a fine scale.Would be great to be able to print with an inkjet or laser printer...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Mongrel Shark
dunno - worth a look at i guess
